title: Why 6,988 Pages of the Tax Code Are Written to Help You Pay Less
guestBio: Barbara Schreihans is the Founder and CEO of Your Tax Coach, creator of the Write Off Your Life™ Course, and a nationally recognized tax strategist who helps entrepreneurs save tens of millions in taxes while growing their profits.
excerpt: Most entrepreneurs overpay taxes because they don't understand that the tax code is actually a roadmap for paying less, not more. Barbara Schreihans reveals how switching business structures and strategic planning can save you thousands.
metaDescription: Learn why only 12 pages of tax code tell you what to pay while 6,988 pages show you how to pay less. Tax strategy tips for women entrepreneurs.
keyTakeaways: Only 12 pages of the 7,000-page tax code tell you what to pay — the other 6,988 pages show you how to pay less.
S-Corps make you 35 times less likely to be audited than sole proprietorships and can save significant self-employment taxes.
Switching to an S-Corp can save you over $6,000 annually even on just $40,000 of income.
Women have higher investment returns than men but invest less money overall.
Your accountant should explain how to take deductions legally, not just say no to everything.
Strategic December car purchases can result in 100% tax write-offs for business vehicles.
Regular money conversations with your spouse prevent financial conflicts and build wealth together.

pullQuotes.text: The tax code is a roadmap for entrepreneurs, not a punishment
Your accountant should tell you HOW, not just say NO
Women have a HIGHER ROI when they invest — we just need to invest more

timestamps.label: Why taxes feel overwhelming for women
The tax code is a roadmap, not a rulebook
When to start thinking strategically about taxes
Most commonly missed deductions and write-offs
Entity structure: LLC vs S-Corp vs C-Corp
Having money dates with your spouse
Mindset shifts around money and investing

content:
If you've ever felt overwhelmed by taxes or convinced that you're "bad with money," this episode will completely shift your perspective. Aimee sits down with Barbara Schreihans, founder of Your Tax Coach, for a conversation that will change how you see the tax code forever.

Here's the truth that most entrepreneurs don't know: the tax code isn't designed to punish you. In fact, it's quite the opposite. While only 12 pages of the massive 7,000-page tax code tell you what you owe, the remaining 6,988 pages are literally instructions for how to pay less. Think about that for a moment. The government has written you a roadmap for keeping more of your hard-earned money, but most business owners never learn how to read it.

The Structure That's Costing You Thousands

One of the biggest mistakes Barbara sees is entrepreneurs staying in business structures that cost them unnecessary taxes. If you're operating as a sole proprietorship or single-member LLC, you're likely paying 15.3% more in self-employment taxes than you need to. Even on just $40,000 of income, switching to an S-Corp election can save you over $6,000 annually.

But here's what makes this even more compelling: S-Corps make you 35 times less likely to be audited than sole proprietorships. You're not just saving money; you're reducing your audit risk significantly. This isn't about being aggressive or pushing boundaries. This is about using the structures the government has created specifically for business owners.

Barbara shares the story of how she used strategic December car purchasing to write off 100% of a $250,000 vehicle. This wasn't some shady scheme - it was following the tax code exactly as written. The key is understanding that business deductions must have a legitimate business purpose, but that purpose can be much broader than most people realize.

Stop Letting Your Accountant Say No

Too many entrepreneurs have accountants who default to "no" instead of explaining how deductions can work legally. Your tax professional should be your strategic partner, not a roadblock. When you ask about writing off travel, meals, or even that designer handbag you use for client meetings, they should explain the requirements and help you document properly rather than shutting down the conversation.

The reality is that almost everything in your life has some business component if you're an entrepreneur. Your home office, your vehicle, your meals with potential clients, your travel to conferences, even your professional wardrobe - these are legitimate business expenses when documented correctly. The key is understanding the rules and following them precisely.

Building Wealth Beyond Deductions

While write-offs get a lot of attention, Barbara emphasizes that true wealth building comes from strategic tax planning combined with smart investing. This is where women have a massive advantage that most don't realize. Studies consistently show that women achieve higher investment returns than men. We're more methodical, less likely to panic-sell during market downturns, and better at long-term planning.

The problem? Women invest significantly less money overall. We're winning the game we're playing, but we're not playing it at the scale we should be. This often comes down to confidence and starting small. Barbara recommends beginning with just $5 per week if that's what feels manageable. The goal is to build the habit and see your money grow, which builds confidence to invest more.

Retirement accounts offer some of the most powerful wealth-building opportunities for business owners. A Solo 401(k) allows you to contribute as both the employee and employer, potentially allowing you to sock away over $60,000 annually in tax-advantaged accounts. For high earners, cash balance plans can allow even larger contributions while reducing current tax liability.

Money Conversations That Don't End in Fights

Since money is the number one cause of divorce, Barbara advocates for regular "money dates" with your spouse. These aren't confrontational budget meetings, but collaborative sessions where you review goals, celebrate wins, and make decisions together. The key is creating a safe space where both partners can share their concerns and dreams without judgment.

Start by reviewing where you are financially, then discuss where you want to be. Make it enjoyable - order takeout from your favorite restaurant, pour some wine if that's your thing, and approach it as planning your future together rather than solving problems. When both partners are involved in financial decisions, you're much more likely to achieve your goals.

The Mindset Shift That Changes Everything

Perhaps the most important message from this conversation is that you are not bad with money. Women are actually excellent with money when we give ourselves permission to learn and engage with it. The narrative that we're not good at finances is cultural conditioning, not reality.

The tax code was written for business owners and entrepreneurs. Every deduction, every strategic opportunity, every wealth-building vehicle discussed in this episode exists because the government wants to encourage business activity and investment. You're not gaming the system by using these strategies - you're participating in the economy exactly as intended.

Your job as a female entrepreneur is to educate yourself, work with qualified professionals who understand strategy, and stop apologizing for wanting to keep more of your money. The tools are there, the opportunities are real, and you absolutely have what it takes to build the wealth you deserve.
